blurb: Abhidhamma texts are systematic summaries and analyses of the teachings drawn from the earlier discourses. The Abhidhamma (spelled abhidharma in Sanskrit) is intended for advanced students who have mastered the teachings of the discourses.

children.translated_name: Systematic Treatises of the Theravāda School
children.blurb: The seven books of the canonical Theravāda Abhidhamma.

children.translated_name: Systematic Treatises of the Dharmaguptaka School
children.blurb: Abhidharma of the Dharmaguptaka school in Chinese translation.

children.translated_name: Systematic Treatises of the Sarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: All seven of the canonical texts of Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma are available in Chinese translation. This complex and sophisticated set of treatises was highly influential in later forms of Buddhism especially from Northern India.

children.translated_name: Systematic Treatises of the Sarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: Texts of the Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma that are available in Sanskrit.

translated_name: Exposition of the Rhinoceros Horn Discourse

blurb: The Itivṛttaka (本事) is a Chinese version of the text found in Pali as the Itivuttaka. The translator is listed as the great Chinese scholar Xuanzang (玄奘) (CE 602–664), also known by his Sanskrit Dharma name Mokṣadeva. In the Taishō edition of the Chinese canon, it is text number 0765, located at T vol. 17, 662c.
A legal issue can only be resolved if all those concerned are present during the process. In addition the procedure must comply with all aspects of monastic law.
If a nun is known for her clarity of memory, any accusations against her may be settled through that nun declaring her innocence based on her memory of the relevant events.
A nun who is insane is not responsible for her own offenses. Any offenses she may have committed while insane can be cleared through a declaration of past insanity.
All offenses, except those entailing expulsion, should be cleared by making amends in the appropriate manner.
A dispute that drags on without resolution may be settled through majority vote.
A nun who is intractable in regard to the offenses she has committed may be subject to an act of censure.

translated_name: 7. Covering over as if with Grass
blurb: If a dispute involves a number of offenses on both sides, and the Order considers that dealing with all the offenses in the usual manner is likely to lead to harshness or schism, then the Order may decide to do a procedure whereby all the offenses are cleared. Offenses that cannot be cleared in this way are those entailing expulsion and suspension, as well as any offenses that involve lay people.

translated_name: 13. The training rule on keeping extra robe-cloth beyond ten days
blurb: A nun who keeps extra robe-cloth for more than ten day must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.
A nun who stays apart from any of her five robes for more than one day must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

translated_name: 15. Keeping extra robe-cloth beyond a month
blurb: A nun may keep extra robe-cloth for a month if she is expecting more. A nun who keeps it beyond that must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

translated_name: 16. Asking an unrelated person for a robe
blurb: A nun who asks a lay person for robe-cloth, except if her robes are lost or destroyed, must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.
When a nun’s robes are lost or destroyed, she should show moderation in asking for robe-cloth. If she does not, she must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

translated_name: 18. Requesting a fine robe from a fund
blurb: If a lay person wants to buy a robe for a nun, and the nun asks them for a fine robe, she must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

translated_name: 19. Requesting a fine robe from a combined fund
blurb: If two separate lay people want to buy a robe for a nun, and the nun asks them to put their money together to buy her a fine robe, she must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

translated_name: 20. Excessive prompting to get a robe
blurb: When money to be used for a nun’s robe has been placed with a steward in the proper way, that nun may hint to the steward that she needs a robe. If she does not get a robe in this way, but she does get one by acting inappropriately, she must relinquish the robe and confess the offense. This rule also prescribes the procedure through which a lay follower comes to hold money on behalf of a donor who wishes to support a nun.
A nun who receives money must relinquish it and confess the offense.
A nun who receives money through trading must relinquish it and confess the offense.
A nun who barters, except with fellow monastics, must relinquish the item received and confess the offense.

translated_name: 24. Exchanging an almsbowl with fewer than five mends
blurb: A nun who asks for a new bowl when her existing bowl is still useable must relinquish it and confess the offense.

translated_name: 25. Storing the five tonics too long
blurb: A nun who keeps tonics for more than seven days must relinquish them and confess the offense.
A nun who gives robe-cloth to another nun, but then takes it back in anger, must relinquish it and confess the offense.
A nun who asks for thread and then has robe-cloth woven with it must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.
If a lay person is having robe-cloth woven for a nun and that nun makes demands of the weavers, then she must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.
A nun who stores “an urgent robe” beyond the acceptable time limit must relinquish it and confess the offense.

translated_name: 30. Diverting an offering intended for the Sangha
blurb: A nun who diverts to herself an offering intended for the Order must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

translated_name: 100. Instructing a lay person to memorize the Teaching
blurb: A nun who teaches an unordained person to learn the word of the Buddha by heart must confess the offense.

translated_name: 101. Lying down with one not fully ordained
blurb: A nun who lies down in the same room as an unordained person for more than three nights must confess the offense.
A nun who lies down in the same room as a man must confess the offense.
A nun who teaches a man more than six sentences of the word of the Buddha must confess the offense.
A nun who tells an unordained person about her own profound spiritual attainment must confess the offense.
A nun who tells an unordained person about another nun’s serious offense must confess the offense.
A nun who digs the earth must confess the offense.
A nun who intentionally destroys vegetation must confess the offense.
A nun who speaks evasively or remains silent when questioned by the Order must confess the offense.
A nun who criticizes or denounces an official of the Order must confess the offense.
A nun who abandons furniture belonging to the Order outdoors must confess the offense.
A nun who abandons bedding in a dwelling belonging to the Order must confess the offense.
A nun who lies down in a dwelling belonging to the Order, encroaching on another nun and aiming to make her depart, must confess the offense.
A nun who throws another nun out of a dwelling belonging to the Order must confess the offense.

translated_name: 114. Sitting down on an upper story
blurb: A nun who uses a bed with unfastened legs on an unplanked upper storey of a hut belonging to the Order must confess the offense.

translated_name: 115. Applying excessive materials to a large dwelling
blurb: A nun who applies more than three layers of building material when building a large dwelling must confess the offense.

translated_name: 116. Pouring water with living beings in it
blurb: A nun who pours water containing living beings onto the ground must confess the offense.

translated_name: 117. Eating excessively at a public guesthouse
blurb: A nun who eats more than one meal at a public alms center must confess the offense.
A nun who eats as part of a specifically invited group of nuns must confess the offense.
A nun who accepts more than three bowlfuls of pastries must confess the offense.
A nun who eats between noon and dawn must confess the offense.
A nun who eats food stored overnight must confess the offense.
A nun who consumes unoffered food or drink must confess the offense.
A nun who takes another nun on almsround, but then tells her to leave because she wants to misbehave must confess the offense.
A nun who sits down intruding on a lustful couple must confess the offense.

translated_name: 125. Sitting with a man in a concealed place
blurb: A nun who sits in a secluded place with a man must confess the offense.
A nun who sits in private with a man must confess the offense.
A nun who visits other lay people before or after an invitational meal must confess the offense.

translated_name: 128. Accepting an invitation of more than four months
blurb: A nun who goes beyond the terms of an invitation to ask for requisites must confess the offense.

translated_name: 129. Going to see a mobilized army
blurb: A nun who goes to see an army in action must confess the offense.

translated_name: 130. Staying more than three days with an army
blurb: A nun who stays with an army for more than three nights must confess the offense.
If, while staying with an army, a nun goes to see the army in action, she must confess the offense.
A nun who consumes alcohol must confess the offense.
A nun who tickles another nun must confess the offense.
A nun who plays in water must confess the offense.
A nun who is disrespectful when rightly corrected about a breach of the monastic code must confess the offense.
A nun who frightens another nun must confess the offense.
A nun who lights a fire to warm herself must confess the offense.
A nun in the central Ganges plain who bathes more than once a fortnight, except when dirty, must confess the offense.

translated_name: 139. Not making stains on a new robe
blurb: A nun who wears a new robe without first marking it must confess the offense.

translated_name: 140. Using assigned property that has not been relinquished
blurb: A nun who uses robe-cloth that has been transferred to a fellow monastic must confess the offense.
A nun who hides the requisites of another nun must confess the offense.
A nun who intentionally kills a living being must confess the offense.

translated_name: 143. Using water with living beings in it
blurb: A nun who uses water that contains living beings must confess the offense.
A nun who tries to reopen an issue of monastic law that has been properly settled must confess the offense.
A nun who travels by arrangement with thieves must confess the offense.

translated_name: 146. Not giving up a bad view
blurb: A nun who says that things declared as obstructive by the Buddha are not actually obstructive must confess the offense.
A nun who lives with a nun who has been ejected for holding the view that things declared as obstructive by the Buddha are not actually obstructive must confess the offense.

translated_name: 148. Supporting a novice nun with a bad view
blurb: A nun who lives with a novice nun who has been expelled for holding the view that things declared as obstructive by the Buddha are not actually obstructive must confess the offense.
A nun who uses a ploy as an excuse for not keeping the monastic code must confess the offense.
A nun who criticizes the monastic code must confess the offense.
A nun who deceptively claims that she did not know a rule in the monastic code must confess the offense.
A nun who in anger strikes another nun must confess the offense.
A nun who in anger physically threatens another nun must confess the offense.
A nun who falsely accuses another nun of an offense entailing suspension must confess the offense.
A nun who intentionally makes another nun anxious must confess the offense.
A nun who eavesdrops on other nuns who are disputing about the Teaching or the monastic law must confess the offense.
A nun who has given her consent for the Order to perform an official act, but then criticizes the act must confess the offense.
A nun who departs without giving her consent while the Order is deciding on an issue must confess the offense.
If a nun, acting as part of a complete Order, gives away a robe to an official of the Order, but then criticizes the act, she must confess the offense.

translated_name: 160. Diverting gains intended for the Sangha
blurb: A nun who diverts to an individual an offering intended for the Order must confess the offense.
A nun who picks up anything considered valuable must confess the offense.
A nun who makes a needle-case of bone, ivory, or horn must destroy it and confess the offense.

translated_name: 163. Making an inappropriate bed or bench
blurb: A nun who makes a bed with long legs must shorten them and confess the offense.

translated_name: 164. Making an upholstered bed or bench
blurb: A nun who makes a bed with luxurious upholstery must remove the upholstery and confess the offense.
A nun who makes a bandage that is too large must cut it down and confess the offense.
A nun who makes a robe that is too large must cut it down and confess the offense.
A nun who lies must confess the offense.
A nun who verbally abuses another nun must confess the offense.
A nun who spreads malicious gossip about another nun must confess the offense.

translated_name: 1. The training rule on sexual intercourse
blurb: A nun who voluntarily has sexual intercourse is expelled.
A nun who steals anything of value is expelled.

translated_name: 3. The training rule on killing a human being
blurb: A nun who kills a human being is expelled.

translated_name: 4. The training rule on falsely claiming a superhuman quality
blurb: A nun who falsely claims a profound spiritual attainment is expelled.

translated_name: 2. Not wearing the upper robe evenly all around
28. Not receiving with attention on the almsbowl
29. Not receiving in the right proportions
30. Not receiving on an even level
32. Not eating with attention on the almsbowl
34. Not eating in the right proportions
14. Causing a schism in the Sangha
blurb: A nun who tries to cause a schism in the Order is to be suspended.
A nun who supports another nun who is trying to cause a schism in the Order is to be suspended.
A nun who refuses to be corrected about breaches of the monastic code is to be suspended.

A nun who causes the lay people in a certain place to lose confidence in the teachings of the Buddha should be banished from that place. If she refuses to accept the banishment, she is to be suspended.
A nun who acts as a go-between for a relationship between a man and a woman is to be suspended.

translated_name: 8. Making a groundless accusation out of anger
blurb: A nun who in anger falsely accuses another nun of an offense entailing expulsion is to be suspended.

translated_name: 9. Making a groundless accusation out of anger, using a pretext
blurb: A nun who in anger uses a ploy to falsely accuse another nun of an offense entailing expulsion is to be suspended.
A legal issue can only be resolved if all those concerned are present during the process. In addition the procedure must comply with all aspects of monastic law.
If a monk is known for his clarity of memory, any accusations against him may be settled through that monk declaring his innocence based on his memory of the relevant events.
A monk who is insane is not responsible for his own offenses. Any offenses he may have committed while insane can be cleared through a declaration of past insanity.
All offenses, except those entailing expulsion, should be cleared by making amends in the appropriate manner.
A dispute that drags on without resolution may be settled through majority vote.
A monk who is intractable in regard to the offenses he has committed may be subject to an act of censure.

translated_name: 7. Covering over as if with Grass
blurb: If a dispute involves a number of offenses on both sides, and the Order considers that dealing with all the offenses in the usual manner is likely to lead to harshness or schism, then the Order may decide to do a procedure whereby all the offenses are cleared. Offenses that cannot be cleared in this way are those entailing expulsion and suspension, as well as any offenses that involve lay people.

The Buddha’s teachings on meditation, morality, the nature of the world, and the path to freedom. These scriptures are our primary sources for the historical Buddha’s life and practice. They depict the Buddha and his students in lively conversation with a diverse range of people.

children.translated_name: Collections of Long Discourses
children.blurb: Collections of “long” discourses and other related texts.

children.translated_name: Collections of Middle Discourses
children.blurb: Collections of “middle-length” discourses and other related texts.

children.translated_name: Collections of Linked Discourses
children.blurb: Collections of “linked” or “connected” discourses and other related texts.

children.translated_name: Collections of Numbered Discourses
children.blurb: Collections of “numbered” or “numerical” discourses and other related texts.

children.translated_name: Collections of Minor Discourses
children.blurb: Collections of sutta texts outside the four main collections.

children.translated_name: Other Discourses
children.blurb: Texts that are similar to those in the previous five collections, but which are difficult to classify.

blurb: The Upāliparipucchā (“Questions of Upāli”) is found as an independent text of the Sarvāstivāda school and in two unaffiliated texts. In other schools, the Upāliparipucchā is incorporated into other works, such as the Uttaragrantha of Mūlasarvāstivāda or the Parivāra of Theravāda.

The texts on Monastic Law (vinaya) detail the lifestyle, rules, and procedures for Buddhist monks and nuns. They provide the guidelines for Buddhist monastics to this day, and in addition, paint a detailed and vivid picture of everyday life in ancient India.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Theravāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Theravāda school in Pali. This collection is followed by mendicants of the Theravāda school. It is the only Vinaya for which we have a complete collection in the original Indic.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Mahāsaṅghika School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Mahāsaṁghika school in Chinese. This school originally flourished in the area of Magadha in India and is now extinct. It was created during the first schism, and therefore its Vinaya has some unique features not found in other schools. Most of the Vinaya texts we have today were brought from Pāṭaliputra in India to China by the Chinese monk Faxian. He then translated them into Chinese together with the Indian monk Buddhabhadra from 416–418 CE.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Mahāsaṅghika School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Mahāsaṁghika school in Sanskrit. This school originally flourished in the area of Magadha in India and is now extinct. It was created during the first schism, and therefore its Vinaya has some unique features not found in other schools. Only the Bhikkhu-pātimokkha has been preserved in Sanskrit.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Lokuttaravāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Lokuttaravāda school in Sanskrit. This is a sub-school of the Mahāsaṁghika and is now extinct. It is not known where the adherents of this school lived, but in the early 7th century, a community existed in Bāmīyan. The text is incomplete, and most of what we have today was discovered in Tibet as palm-leaf manuscripts by Tripiṭakācārya Śri Rāhula Sānkṛtyāyana between 1935–1938. It is of special importance for bhikkhunī Vinaya studies, since it has the only complete Bhikkhunī-vibhaṅga in an Indian language besides the Pāli.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Mahīśāsaka School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Mahīśāsaka school in Chinese. This school originated in the area of Avanti in India but then spread throughout India and to Sri Lanka. It is now extinct. Most of the Vinaya texts we have today were brought from the Abhayagiri Vihāra in Sri Lanka to China by the Chinese monk Faxian. They were translated from Sanskrit into Chinese by the Kashmiri monk Buddhajīva together with Zhu Daosheng in 434 CE.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Dharmaguptaka School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Dharmaguptaka school in Chinese. This school originally flourished in North-western India and had a large influence on Central Asia and China. Monastics in East Asia follow this Vinaya. Most of the Vinaya texts we have today were translated from 410–412 CE by the Kashmiri monk Buddhayaśas, who recited the entire text from memory, together with Zhu Fonian.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Dharmaguptaka School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Dharmaguptaka school in Gandhari. This school originally flourished in North-western India and had a large influence on Central Asia and China. Monastics in East Asia follow this Vinaya.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Sarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Sarvāstivāda school in Chinese. This school spread throughout North India and Central Asia. The Sarvāstivāda Vinaya was the most common Vinaya in China before they changed to the Dharmaguptaka. The Sarvāstivāda school is now extinct. Most of the Vinaya texts we have today were translated from about 404–409 CE by the Kashmiri monks Kumārajīva and Puṇyatāra. When Puṇyatāra died, Dharmaruci replaced him and finished the translation.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Sarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Sarvāstivāda school in Sanskrit. This school spread throughout North India and Central Asia. The Sarvāstivāda Vinaya was the most common Vinaya in China before they changed to the Dharmaguptaka. The Sarvāstivāda school is now extinct.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Mūlasarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Mūlasarvāstivāda school in Chinese. The debate about the origins of this school is still ongoing but it is clear that it had a large influence on Central Asia. Monastics of the Tibetan tradition follow this Vinaya. Most of the Vinaya texts in Chinese we have today were translated between 695 and 713 CE by Yijing. We also have a full Vinaya translation in Tibetan, and a part of the original Sanskrit.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Mūlasarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Mūlasarvāstivāda school in Sanskrit. The debate about the origins of this school is still ongoing but it is clear that it had a large influence on Central Asia. Monastics of the Tibetan tradition follow this Vinaya. The Sanskrit version of this Vinaya is only partially preserved. Full texts survive in Chinese and Tibetan translation.

children.translated_name: Monastic Law of the Mūlasarvāstivāda School
children.blurb: The Monastic Law for the Mūlasarvāstivāda school in Tibetan. The debate about the origins of this school is still ongoing but it is clear that it had a large influence on Central Asia. Monastics of the Tibetan tradition follow this Vinaya. Besides the Tibetan text, we also have a full Vinaya translation in Chinese, and a part of the original Sanskrit.

children.translated_name: Other Texts of Monastic Law
children.blurb: These are some individual texts that do not belong to any of the schools above. They are either from schools whose Vinaya texts have been mostly lost, or are works that have not yet been attributed to a school.
